one_line_takeaway: A dual-domain (spatial bi-directional attention + DCT frequency attention) deepfake detector whose novel wave-like 'feature superposition' fusion pushes cross-manipulation and cross-dataset generalization above the CADDM baseline (up to +6.15% AUC), at the cost of weaker low-quality-video performance and a few cross-manipulation regressions.
key_finding: On the FF++(HQ) cross-manipulation protocol D2Fusion improves AUC over its CADDM baseline by up to +6.15% (train NeuralTextures / test FaceSwap: 80.75 vs 74.60) and by +4.05/+2.04/+0.34% on other NT-trained transfers, while cross-dataset generalization (train FF++(HQ)) reaches AUC 88.14 (CD1), 83.29 (CD2), 74.93 (DFDC), and 90.40 (DFR) — the best or near-best among compared methods, with a +4.00% DFR gain over CADDM. Ablations confirm all three modules contribute, with the largest cross-dataset lift on DFR (fine-grained spectral +7.78% AUC, feature superposition up to +10.61% AUC). Gains come at modest cost: +8.47M params and +2.29G FLOPs over ResNet-34.
core_contribution: D2Fusion is a Deepfake (facial-video forgery) detection framework built on a ResNet-34 backbone that fuses spatial- and frequency-domain artifact cues. Its three OWN contributions are: (1) a bi-directional attention module that applies horizontal and vertical average pooling (kernels (1,W) and (H,1)) plus 1x1 convolutions to encode local positional information and localize manipulated facial regions in the spatial domain; (2) a fine-grained spectral attention module that channel-splits features into n parts and applies per-part Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) multi-spectral partitioning to retain high-frequency texture/edge (global) forgery cues; and (3) a 'feature superposition' fusion strategy that recasts fused tokens as wave-like tokens with learned amplitude (channel-FC) and phase (channel-FC), then performs phase-aware token-fusion so that same-phase (authentic vs authentic / artifact vs artifact) tokens reinforce and opposite-phase tokens cancel, amplifying the authentic-vs-artifact separation. NOTE: the multi-scale facial-swap data-augmentation module and the multi-scale detection shortcut are borrowed from CADDM (ref [32], which is also the paper's baseline), and the DSSIM-guided sliding window is prior work; these are NOT the paper's contribution.
threat_model: The paper is a defense (detector), not an attack. It targets the deepfake-forgery threat as framed in its own introduction: adversaries use AI face-swapping (auto-encoder-based) and face-reenactment (GAN-based, e.g. NeuralTextures) to produce realistic fake facial videos for financial fraud, revenge pornography, fake political news, and reputation damage. The security-relevant design goal is GENERALIZATION: detecting unseen manipulation techniques and cross-dataset/real-world distributions, since prior detectors overfit to operation-specific artifacts. The paper does not model an adaptive adversary attacking the detector itself (no adversarial-robustness evaluation), though it notes some cited single-domain methods are vulnerable to adversarial attacks.
limitations: 1) Performance degrades on high-compression / low-quality (LQ) FF++ videos due to loss of forgery clues; the paper's own Limitation section and Figure 13 failure cases show samples detected in HQ/RAW but missed in LQ. 2) Not uniformly SOTA: on FF++(HQ) intra-dataset AUC it trails IDM by 0.28, and cross-manipulation shows real regressions vs CADDM (-3.52% train FS/test F2F; -2.68% train DF/test FS; also -1.60%, -1.49%), attributed to overfitting to over-represented manipulation types. 3) No adversarial-robustness evaluation despite motivating the work partly by other methods' adversarial vulnerability. 4) Frame-level only (no temporal modeling), and some baseline numbers are reproduced by the authors under their own setting. 5) The wave/phase 'quantum measurement' framing is heuristic; the mechanism is standard channel-FC operations rather than actual quantum computation.

datasets_used: FaceForensics++ (FF++) with LQ/HQ/RAW compression and four sub-methods DF/F2F/FS/NT: 1000 real + 4000 fake videos
Deepfake Detection Challenge (DFDC): 19154 real + 100000 fake videos
DeeperForensics-1.0 (DFR): 50000 real + 10000 fake videos (60000 total, 17.6M frames)
quantitative_results: Intra-dataset FF++ (Table 3), Ours ACC/AUC: LQ 91.43/93.33, HQ 97.77/99.42, RAW 99.60/99.83
Intra-dataset HQ AUC: Ours 99.42 trails IDM 99.74 by 0.28; beats CADDM 99.24 and FADE 99.31

Cross-manipulation FF++(HQ) AUC (Table 4): largest gain +6.15% over CADDM on train NT/test FS (80.75 vs 74.60); other NT-trained gains +4.05% (F2F), +2.04% (DF), +0.34% (NT); regressions -3.52% (FS->F2F), -2.68% (DF->FS)
Multi-source: Ours exceeds MAT by 14.62 in P on F2F; exceeds FreqNet by 12.02/3.82/7.75 in P/R/F1 on NT
Cross-dataset AUC (Table 6), train FF++(HQ): Ours CD1 88.14, CD2 83.29, DFDC 74.93, DFR 90.40; +4.00% over CADDM on DFR; exceeds LSDA by 1.33 on DFDC
Ablation (Table 7/8): fine-grained spectral module adds +1.63% (FF++), +2.10% (CD1), +2.40% (CD2), +3.07% (DFDC), +7.78% (DFR) AUC; feature superposition adds up to +10.61%/+4.60% AUC on DFR
Network load (Table 9): Ours 45.66M params (+8.47M over ResNet-34), 5.97G FLOPs (+2.29G), ACC 97.77 vs ResNet-34 96.20 and CADDM 97.59
